content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement
(916) 322-5662         322-5660            322-5901         322-6441

October 3, 1984


Stephen E. Lenzi
Senior Counsel
Automobile Club of Southern California
1225 Eighth Street, Suite 475
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re: Your Request for Advice,
    Our Advice No. A-84-228

Dear Mr. Lenzi:

    You have requested our advice on the following question:

FACTS

    Periodically the Automobile Club will host dinners for
independent tow service operators who contract with the Club
to provide emergency road service. The dinners are expressions of appreciation and goodwill in recognition of the
operators' service. In attendance are Auto Club management and supervisors for the geographical area involved.
Occasionally, members of the California Highway Patrol
(CHP) from the local area and their spouses will attend in
recognition that local CHP management and the tow operators
have a mutual interest in traffic operations, and the need
to maintain working relationships. Usually there are 25 to
175 persons in attendance at these events.

QUESTION

    As an employer of a lobbyist, must the Automobile Club
include the cost of the dinners provided to members of the
CHP and their spouses on the quarterly report required by
Section 86109 of the Government Code (and attendant
regulations)?

    If the answer is yes, must the cost of the entire event
be reported, or only the specific costs of the dinners
provided to officers and their spouses?

Stephen E. Lenzi
October 3, 1984
Page 2


ANALYSIS

As a lobbyist employer, the Automobile Club has a responsibility to report "payments to influence legislative or administrative action." Government Code Section 86109(c).1/

If the Automobile Club is seeking to influence "administrative action" by the CHP, as defined in Section 82002,2/ then it must report its expenditures in this regard. Expenditures for gifts such as dinners must be reported if the officials who receive the dinners are "agency officials" within the meaning of Section 82004.3/ If reporting is required, the Automobile Club must report the total cost of the event, the pro rata benefit to each "agency official" in attendance and his or her name, and the name of the spouse of each such official in attendance, together with the pro rata benefit to the spouse.

Should you have further questions in this regard, you should address them to Jeanne Pritchard in our Technical Assistance and Analysis Division at 916/322-5662.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel, Legal Division

REL:km
Enclosure

cc: Jeanne Pritchard

_____________________

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified. Note changes in this section made by AB 2541, Chap. 161, Stats. 1984, effective January 1, 1985.

2/ See also, regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18202 (copy enclosed).

3/ Note that a member of the Highway Patrol is only considered an "agency official" if he or she participates in "administrative actions" as defined in Section 82002.

Automobile Club of Southern California

1225 EIGHTH STREET, SUITE 475, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

LEGAL DIVISION

August 30, 1984


Ms. Barbara Milman
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms. Milman:

Re: Opinion Request

Your opinion regarding the following is respectfully requested:

Periodically the Automobile Club will host dinners for independent tow service operators who contract with the Club to provide emergency road service. The dinners are expressions of appreciation and goodwill in recognition of the operators' service. In attendance are Auto Club management and supervisors for the geographical area involved. Occasionally, members of the California Highway Patrol (CHP) from the local area and their spouses will attend in recognition that local CHP management and the tow operators have a mutual interest in traffic operations, and the need to maintain working relationships. Usually there are 25 to 175 persons in attendance at these events.

As an employer of a lobbyist, must the Automobile Club include the cost of the dinners provided to members of the CHP and their spouses on the quarterly report required by Section 86109 of the Government Code (and attendant regulations)?

If the answer is yes, must the cost of the entire event be reported, or only the specific costs of the dinners provided to the officers and their spouses?

Ms. Barbara Milman
Page 2
August 30, 1984


We have a concern that the dollars represented in hosting
several of these dinners will dramatically increase the
amount reported by the Club as "payments to influence,"
when in fact it will be very misleading to the general
public.

Your consideration of this matter is sincerely appreciated.

Sincerely,

[signature]
STEPHEN E. LENZI
Senior Counsel

SEL:mib

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • •    Administration • •    Executive/Legal • •    Enforcement
(916) 322-5662          322-5660            322-5901          322-6441


September 10, 1984


Stephen E. Lenzi
Senior Counsel
Automobile Club of
Southern California
1225 - 8th Street, Suite 475
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                        Re:  Your Request for Advice,
                                             Advice No. A-84-228

Dear Mr. Lenzi:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission.  If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        General Counsel

BAM:km

include the cost of the dinners provided to members of the

regulations)?

provided to the officers and their spouses?

October 3, 1984
sections.conclusion: If the Automobile Club is seeking to influence "administrative action" by the CHP, as defined in Section 82002, then it must report its expenditures in this regard. Expenditures for gifts such as dinners must be reported if the officials who receive the dinners are "agency officials" within the meaning of Section 82004. If reporting is required, the Automobile Club must report the total cost of the event, the pro rata benefit to each "agency official" in attendance and his or her name, and the name of the spouse of each such official in attendance, together with the pro rata benefit to the spouse.

sections.question_synthetic: Must the Automobile Club, as a lobbyist employer, report the cost of dinners provided to California Highway Patrol members and their spouses on quarterly lobbying expenditure reports, and if so, should the total event cost or only specific dinner costs be reported?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Automobile Club must report dinner expenditures if they are intended to influence CHP administrative action and the recipients are agency officials. If reporting is required, the full event cost and pro rata benefits to each agency official and their spouses must be reported with names.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes both initial intake letter (Sept 10, 1984) and substantive FPPC response (Oct 3, 1984). Question and conclusion sections clearly marked in the response. High confidence extraction; minor formatting irregularities in source document do not affect content clarity.

QUESTION: As an employer of a lobbyist, must the Automobile Club 
include the cost of the dinners provided to members of the 
CHP and their spouses on the quarterly report required by 
Section 86109 of the Government Code (and attendant 
regulations)? 
If the answer is yes, must the cost of the entire event 
be reported, or only the specific costs of the dinners 
provided to the officers and their spouses? 


Stephen E. Lenzi 
October 3, 1984

CONCLUSION: If the Automobile Club is seeking to influence "administrative action" by the CHP, as defined in Section 82002, then it must report its expenditures in this regard. Expenditures for gifts such as dinners must be reported if the officials who receive the dinners are "agency officials" within the meaning of Section 82004. If reporting is required, the Automobile Club must report the total cost of the event, the pro rata benefit to each "agency official" in attendance and his or her name, and the name of the spouse of each such official in attendance, together with the pro rata benefit to the spouse.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises the Automobile Club that it must report the costs of dinners provided to CHP members and spouses as lobbying expenditures if intended to influence administrative action, requiring disclosure of total event costs and pro rata benefits with attendee names.
